sentences.text: Yeah, I saw this.
And, of course, we saw most of the Russian personnel basically getting out of town as fast as they could.
Because there was a problem being that we were so close to the border.
And the German armies actually by then probably, sort of, bypassed our town.
They were probably ahead on the sides, so there was, like, a small window of opportunity to get out.
And you could get out still because we were not far from the city [POLISH], which was a railroad sort of junction, and if you got there, you were able to get on a train and still make it to Russia.
We knew-- we knew that war broke out, that the Germans attacked-- attacked Russia.

Of course, we did not know the significance of what was going to happen to Jews, although by then, we should have known because by 1941, the Germans already did a job on the Polish Jews that was under their occupation.
They already had a ghetto, I believe, in Lublin at that point, and they were already persecuting and killing Jews in a lesser way in the rest of Poland and Germany.
Then-- it took a few days, and then suddenly, the Germans were marching into our town.
I remember being on that street.
They were coming in on the main street that goes between [POLISH] and [POLISH] and [POLISH].
And I saw a little table, and the rabbi-- the rabbi and another man sitting at the table with bread and salt, which was a traditional way of welcoming, like, an invading army.
And the Germans were marching in the middle of the street, the main army.
And one of the officers broke rank and ran over to that little table and kicked it over, and told the rabbi and the other man to get out of there.
And I didn't-- I couldn't understand.
I didn't know what the significance, what-- what it meant, but it was sort of a scary and unfriendly act.
And I ran-- I ran away from there, you know, when I saw that.
And and that army marched-- most of it marched past town.
Some of them marched-- there was a big-- there was a monastery that also had, like, a high school before the war.
It was mainly, like, Catholic.
And when the Russians came in, they made their headquarters there.
It had a lot of ground area.
So now the Germans took over the Russian headquarters and they set up in the same place.
A lot of these soldiers marched there.
And then the soldiers disappeared, basically, from the streets.
In a very short period of time, a new group moved into town.
They came in on motorcycles with machine guns and trucks.
Not many trucks.

And then we found out that those were what they call the Einsatzgruppen, that were SS-- SS groups that are specifically trained to follow the main army right behind the main army and kill as many civilians as they wanted to, like, in a first sweep.
And although they didn't specify Jews at that point, they-- their objective was political prisoners and political people, anybody accused of being a communist, a communist sympathizer, and Jews.
We still didn't-- maybe some of the adults, maybe, had a different feeling, but basically, there was some fear, but I don't think we knew exactly what we were fearing.
At that point we still went out.

Maybe not play per se, we were still out on the street because the atmosphere was already tense, and they did have German, you know, policing the streets, even though you didn't have the main army body there.
Well, what happened is as soon as the SS moved in, they issued an order that all Jewish males from 16 to 60 should report to the center of town in order to change passports.
That was part-- that's when they--
Yeah, that's the same group.
The Einsatzgruppen are basically the SS that came in with-- with that purpose.
And whoever doesn't report would be shot.
So the next morning, most-- many of the people reported.
And for some reason again, I was there.
No.
He was too young yet.
He wasn't old enough.
You had to be, you know, 16 and up.
And a lot of people that had to go didn't go because the feeling was that it's not a good feeling.
So the men reported, and when I got there, I saw men lined up in the middle of the street in rows of maybe 8 or 10 and down the road.
And I remember looking at one particular man who was a friend of our family, and his face was so pale-- like, you could see, practically read, the fright in his face.
And then I noticed the German-- you know, they had the SS there with machine guns.
They had what I found out later was gestapo with brown-- brown collars and brown insignia.
And they had soldiers and officers in black uniforms, which were the SS or, like, the secret police.
And there was a car that was almost like a limo, except the military-- a military version, that must have brought in a very high-ranking officer that was there.
And once the people were lined up there, they couldn't get away, and they were already watched with machine guns.
And I got scared.
When I saw that, I somehow-- you know, the atmosphere was very frightening.
And I got out of there, and then I noticed they marched-- they marched those people to the German headquarters.
And then later on, I heard shots coming from that area.
The Germans later-- some people were let go that had specific crafts, specific type of workers.
Later, the Germans let it be known that those people were taken away for labor, to a labor camp, and that within a year, they're going to return.
To me it was obvious what was happening there when I heard the shots.
Later on, I saw Ukrainian men going through the back, through, like, the meadow area with shovels.
Some of the mothers and wives and sisters chose to believe that that was true-- that they-- they them for work and that they're going to come back.
In 1993 when I went back to put up monuments in the town, one of the monuments we put up was for these 400 men that were shot on that day.
And then that-- you know, that happened and it was over with.
And then the SS disappeared from town.
Well, everybody was still home at that point.
Only the men that left left, you know?
But the families, all the families, and the children they didn't bother at that point and the women they didn't bother at that point.
And the SS left town, and they continued to the next town.
The same thing was happening in all the towns in that particular area of [POLISH].
Well, the conversations were that some thought that they killed people there, and some thought that they actually took them away because the Germans had the ability to engineer a situation.
They made a boy write some postcards in Yiddish and send them back to town.
And-- and supposedly, trucks were seen leaving with people, and telling the families that they were taken away for work, and that everything is OK.
No.
So now-- now things were-- if you called it normal.
I mean, now life was going on again and people were getting up again and going through the day and living.
The Germans ordered a curfew for Jews.
So now after 6:00 in the evening, no Jew could be out on the street.
Anybody caught in the street at that time could be shot.
So we would make sure that we are in the house after 6:00, and close the-- pull the window shades and close up the windows to make sure.
Because German patrols used to patrol the street, and there were two of them.
And you could hear them already one street away because they were marching on the cobblestones in step.
So they went ka-lunk, ka-lunk, you know?
So I knew already when they were coming.
And I guess I was a sneaky kid, and I used to like to peek out and, you know, watch them.
And they were dressed, basically, in war gear with helmets and bayonets and hand grenades hanging on the side.
And so it was a frightening, frightening situation.
And then and then they organized the judenrat.

So the Germans, instead of giving people orders individually, they would-- they would tell the-- judenrat was they would come in maybe to the rabbi and say, by tomorrow 12:00, I want to have so many people forming a judenrat or a committee that will represent the Jews, and if not, you'll be shot.

So it was not-- most of the time and in most cases, it was not a glorious job that everybody ran to grab, although there were some abuses, maybe, in other towns, you know, that I read about or hear about.

But most people did not want to be involved in that because if something happened and the judenrat rather tried to represent the Jews, and let's say protested, so they took them and shot them and they-- they said, form another one.
So but now, the judenrat would order-- let's say the Germans wanted 50 people, 100 people to do work.
They would have to provide them, and that was on a daily basis.
Now, they would also-- the Germans would also go, suddenly appear in town, and-- and whoever was Jewish-- and, of course, the Jews had to wear bands.
Do you remember getting a band?
I didn't have to wear it.
My brother and sister already wore it.
So it's interesting.
We didn't have running water in our house, but there was a pump about a block away, so we had to go to get water.
And we already knew the fear was there that you can be captured any time.
So being that they were wearing-- we had two kinds of situations.
The white bands were the star of David, and they also made us wear yellow circles in the front and in the back.

And also, every Jewish home had to be-- had to be marked with a yellow circle or maybe larger, maybe 8 inches in diameter, so they in a way knew which were the Jewish homes, and they knew if you walked on the streets, they knew who was Jewish.
So they would just grab people and then take them to do work, and it could be-- it could be sweeping the streets, or it could be to the headquarters to do work, or whatever, you know?
And then at the end of the day towards late afternoon, they would let them go.
What were you starting to say about with going for the water?
You were in the middle of a thought.
So being that-- that's one of my habits.
I jump to other things.
That's OK.
You have a lot of things-- Yeah, other people tell me the same thing.
So I would volunteer to go to get the water because I didn't wear the--
I was too-- I was too young to wear it.
I think they started with 12.
And I was 10 at that point, you know?
So I didn't have to-- I didn't have to wear it, so I used to volunteer to go to get the water.
And it was heavy, you know, with the pails.
You had to carry it.
But then I learned that if you balance out with two pails, it's much easier than with one pail that unbalances you.
So it was-- it was a--
--a tense situation.
Well, not as much as my brother would be afraid or my sister because they were already older, that they could be grabbed for labor or for whatever, you know?
Or to be killed.
You know, the atmosphere was tense.
No, the stores were already closed.
You know, in other words, the stores were closed up during the Russian occupation.
So basically, most Jews had nothing to it was difficult to make a living already.
So you had to buy and sell, and sort of black market if you had some things to sell.
Farmers would still come into town.
Like, we had this particular farm family named [POLISH].
[POLISH] means 'plum' in Polish, so it was an easy name to remember.
And maybe that name saved my life later on.
This particular family would come to our house, and they would bring butter and cheese and eggs and my mother would give them-- give them some materials to make a dress or a suit.
So there was a bartering type of situation that sort of helped us get food.
And most people had that-- had a problem with it, and that was one way of doing it somehow.
Or doing some work still and maybe making some money, not directly because Jews were not allowed to have businesses or to do anything.
And then the winter approached.
And I remember-- I remember once there was a snowstorm, and there was a lot of snow piled up on the sides.
And in our town, when the snow came, you don't-- well, we didn't have cars per se, you know, so it was either wagons on wheels, and then during the winter, there were sleds.
You know, horse-pulled sleds.
So I saw one of my teachers-- religious, Hebrew teacher, who must have been in the 70s, who was walking.
And the older people wore, like, the hats were, like, Hasidic.
Actually, even though they were Hasidic, they were actually Russian czarist.
They were round with one of these things in the front.
He was walking, and most of the older people wore these long coats, you know?

And by chance, a sled with two German officers was coming right opposite to where he was walking, and when they saw him-- and, of course he was an old man and had a beard and payots, you know-- they stopped.
They stopped, and one of them got off.
And I was maybe, like, a half a block away, and he was probably saying things to him.
Like they normally would insult Jews-- [GERMAN].
Suddenly-- suddenly, he pushed him into the snow, and the old man fell.
And his hat fell off.
And he made him put snow into the hat and put it on him, and abused him some more.
The other officer was saying already, come on, let's go, like, that's enough, you know.
And he got back on the sled and they continued.
That was the direction of the headquarters.
And the old man got himself up and cleaned out his hat and went home.
It so happens later on towards the end when they kill everybody, I end up hiding in a hiding place in this man's home, and I was there when they captured everybody, and I managed to get away.

And then another thing I saw was this-- another incident happened before that where one of the Germans that was patrolling at night somehow had an encounter towards late evening, after the curfew, with the only photographer in town.
We had a photographer who probably took all those pictures that I have from our town-- was taken by him because there was only one photographer with a studio that did all this stuff.
And there was some argument between this German soldier.
Now, he was not an SS.

He was still-- there was a-- there was a group of soldiers stationed also in town to do certain things, and I can see why because the SS were busy doing, you know, what they thought important things-- killing people and so on.
Massacring.
So that soldier was drunk, and he ran into some misunderstanding with the photographer and stabbed him to death.

From what I understand, that particular commandant or the head of that military group in town, who was an older German, possibly of Austrian background and was there during the First World War, the Austro-Hungary empire.
And he was captured as prisoner and was stationed in our town for some reason, as prisoner he was there.
And the Jews of the town somehow helped him.
They gave him food and gave him-- so he sort of-- that soldier was punished, the one that stabbed the man.
And that was the first killing in town.
And whether it's true or not-- I hardly believe that that would be true-- that soldier was executed.
But I have my doubts whether they would actually kill him, but that's possible.
No, no, but this was seen by people that I spoke-- yeah, that was a fact.
Well, you began to feel that, you know, any German can go into any-- which they could at that point-- to any Jewish home and basically do what they wanted to.
The next thing I saw was we had not many, like, real Hasidim that would wear the fur hats like the very Hasidic people do.
In our town, only the rabbi wore a hat like that.
But there was somebody married, a person like that, who lived in the town.
He was a young man.
And he-- again, it was his luck to walk on my street, like, one house away.
And suddenly I saw this German soldier with his rifle butt, you know, hitting that man over the head.
And the man collapsed, cursing at him.
And eventually the soldier left, and he managed to get up.
And he only lived, like, a block away.
And I saw the way he was walking, like, he was having trouble.
He made it back to his house, and he died the next day.
Well, it just didn't happen suddenly.
So now, you know, it's a process.
The next thing that happens is that we-- somehow we get the feeling that something is going to happen.
And that was-- that was in the beginning of '42.
And so at that point, we thought it was serious, so my mother, my sister, my brother, and I went to our grandparents' house, my father's parents' house, and they made a hiding place.
It was on-- it was on one of the streets going from-- from [POLISH] to [POLISH], and it was, like, a little bit out of the city already.
It was, like, in a suburb area.
And they were probably the only Jewish house at that particular spot.
So they made a hiding place in an attic, and when we got there, everybody got up in the attic.
That consisted of three of my aunts, my father's sisters, my sister, and my mother.
And my grandfather was very old and sick, and at that point, they still didn't-- sort of theoretically didn't-- take older men and young children.
So my grandfather and my brother and I slept downstairs in a room.
In the middle of the night-- actually, not in the middle of the night.
We went to sleep early because it was a curfew and we couldn't light any candles or light inside.
One thing, we didn't have any, probably.
Two, we didn't want to attract anybody from the outside.
So it must have been late at night, and it was very dark.
And I heard German officers right across the street.
There was a Ukrainian family living there, and they had young girls.
So the officers, they were sitting outside and laughing and talking to those girls.
Somehow they managed to communicate between the German and the Ukrainian because most Ukrainians didn't speak German.
And suddenly I hear the German voices approaching close to the house.
And then suddenly I see a flash light shining inside the house on the ceiling.
And they were, like, looking to see maybe there was something open in the attic, going up to an attic.
And then they walked around to the side where the door was and they started knocking on the door and yelling [INAUDIBLE].

[INAUDIBLE] was my grandfather's name, and for some reason they knew the name, which tells me that the neighbors, you know, told them what the name was, and that the neighbors may have told them that-- because the Ukrainians kept score.

They knew who was captured already and who was taken away already, you know, so they knew that they haven't-- this was already a day or two after they started this particular sort of action against Jews, where now they were going to the homes and they were-- they were taking Jews out and they were taking them away and shooting them.
Some of them they already shot at those land mines and some in other places.
So they're knocking on the door, and they-- We'll continue in the second.
